title: Telemarketing Bureaus and Other Contact Centers

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561422 ($17,861,129,852) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561422 ($32,736,522,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Mail Clerks and Mail Machine Operators, Except Postal Service

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $84,840 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561422); class <5: 926 firms, avg revenue/firm $563,971 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $88,261 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 247 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,328,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $207,931 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 210 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,113,986 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,339 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,157,090 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $807,085 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 201 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,411,512 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,820,402 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 315 firms, avg revenue/firm $77,024,206 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,054,288 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 201
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Core exposure: AI voice/chat agents handle routine tier-1 interactions end-to-end. Sized from deflection evidence, not assumed: Gartner-cited data shows AI deflecting 45%+ of queries, and production voice-AI deployments grew 340% YoY; adding handle-time compression on residual human calls (agent-assist, auto-summarization) puts the substitutable share of CSR labor cost at ~0.65. Residual: escalation, empathy-critical, complex multi-system cases.

Scripted outbound qualification is technically highly automatable, but the FCC's Feb 2024 TCPA ruling makes AI-generated voices in robocalls illegal without prior express consent - a legal limiter on full automation of cold outbound. Consented and warm outbound automates.
Payment reminders, promise-to-pay workflows and scripted negotiation automate well; FDCPA compliance actually favors consistent bots. Complex disputes and hardship conversations persist.
QA scoring is already AI-saturated (100% interaction scoring vs sampling) and coaching analytics automate; span of control widens. Live escalation handling and people management persist.
Enterprise BPO selling is relationship/RFP work; proposal and RFP-response production automates as a sliver.
Workforce management, forecasting and reporting automate substantially; client relationship management and site leadership persist.
Likely 4-digit proxy artifact; routine transactional clerical work is largely automatable where it genuinely exists in contact centers.
Proxy artifact from sibling document-prep codes; speech-to-text transcription is essentially solved by current AI.

breakdown_by_role.role: Mail Clerks and Mail Machine Operators (43-9051)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proxy artifact; physical mail handling is not AI-substitutable, but digitization/sorting workflow slivers automate.
Tier-1 desktop/telephony support automates; CCaaS/AI platform integration work grows with deployment.

The provided role_mix is a 4-digit 5614 proxy in which agent occupations are diluted; per prompt guidance, agent occupations plausibly run 55-70% of the wage bill at 561422 proper, so the large residual is mostly additional frontline agent-type labor plus routine clerical, with some non-automatable facilities/training/HR. Blended at 0.55, slightly below the CSR fraction.

pi_dist.rationale: Pricing-model deflation is the central mechanism: outsourced contact-center work is billed per agent-hour, per FTE, per minute, or per transaction, so when AI deflects contacts or cuts handle time the billed unit itself disappears - AI savings flow to the client as fewer billed units, not to the operator as margin. Enterprise clients own the customer data and telephony stack, run multi-vendor RFPs on 2-3 year contracts, and rebid competitively. Outcome/resolution-based re-pricing exists but is nascent (Everest Group tracks the shift; voice-heavy operators' compressed public multiples evidence the market's view that operators are not keeping the spread). Small-bureau SMB clients are stickier and less sophisticated, which keeps the value above the floor.

pi_moat.rationale: No license, statute, or physical act stands between the client and an AI voice agent doing the same job: clients can license AI agents directly from CCaaS/AI vendors (Genesys, NICE, Five9, model providers) and cut the bureau out. Real but modest barriers: compliance certifications (HIPAA, PCI), state-licensed collections operations, domain-trained workflows and integrations, and switching costs on live programs. Domestic bureaus already lost the cost-arbitrage war to offshore once, evidencing weak defenses against a cheaper substitute.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~40% of firms materially using AI (large operators near-universally deploying agent-assist/deflection in production; micro bureaus lagging). Trajectory: production voice-AI deployments +340% YoY, and CCaaS platforms now bundle AI agents into standard offerings, so small bureaus acquire AI passively through their existing vendor stack. Remaining ~10pp to reach 50% of firms at the observed pace = ~1-1.5 years. Historical analog (cloud CCaaS ~8-10 years to majority) is a ceiling, not a guide - AI rides on the already-installed CCaaS rails rather than requiring a platform migration. t50 = 1.5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: CloudTalk AI Voice Agent Statistics 2026 (aggregating industry surveys incl. ContactBabel data); Gartner deflection data via Lorikeet
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: "78% of businesses are now deploying voice AI"; "production deployments grew 340% year-over-year across 500+ organisations"; ContactBabel-cited economics: "AI voice minutes cost around $0.08/min versus $7.16 per human-handled call." Material = AI voice/chat deflection or full agent-assist live in production on client programs (not pilots). Caveat per prompt trap: most surveys cover in-house centers and enterprise buyers, not BPO operators; firm-count-weighted adoption across 2,299 mostly small 561422 firms estimated at 0.40 (large operators near-universal, micro bureaus lagging).

historical_analogs.value: Cloud CCaaS took roughly 8-10 years from early-2010s introduction to ~50% of US contact-center seats (~2020-21); IVR took ~15 years to near-universality in the 1980s-90s. Gen-AI agent adoption is tracking far faster (340% YoY production growth) because it deploys on top of the installed CCaaS base rather than requiring platform migration.
historical_analogs.source: CloudTalk / CallBotics aggregation of ContactBabel and industry deployment data
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Voice AI 'becoming operational infrastructure rather than experimental pilots'; production deployments +340% YoY. CCaaS/IVR timelines are estimated from industry history, not a single published series.

owners_60plus_pct.source: US Census Bureau, Annual Business Survey - generic small-business owner age distribution (industry-specific owner-age data for 561422 does not exist openly; per prompt, estimated from generic small-business data)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: "Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over" - owners 55+ account for ~51% of employer businesses (2019 ABS). Interpolating the 55-64/65+ split puts owners 60+ at roughly 30% of employer-business owners; applied as the estimate for small contact-center bureau owners (range 0.25-0.40).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No industry-specific documentation of a succession shortage for contact-center bureaus was found; small-operator demographics are thin in this industry (as the prompt anticipated), and weak buyer demand for small voice-heavy bureaus amid AI uncertainty is a demand problem, not a documented owner-succession shortage. Bonus not applied.

active_consolidators.source: Site Selection Group 'The BPO Consolidation Wave Has Begun'; PRNewswire (MCI/Market Force); ResultsCX press release (ChrysCapital)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed platforms with verified add-ons in the last 24 months: MCI (acquired Market Force Information, Mar 2024), ResultsCX (ChrysCapital bought platform from One Equity Partners, 2024; ~20,000 employees, 25 centers), IntouchCX (acquired WebPurify, Jan 2025), Everise (Warburg Pincus-backed, active), ibex/other mid-tier CX platforms. Site Selection Group: 'The BPO consolidation wave has begun... valuations across traditional voice-heavy BPO platforms have compressed amid AI uncertainty.' Conservative count of named, verified-active PE-backed consolidators = 5.

buy_mult.source: First Page Sage small-business EBITDA multiples 2025 (size-tier benchmarks for labor-intensive services); Site Selection Group compression commentary
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Labor-intensive services benchmark: 'a $2M EBITDA staffing company sells at 4x-5x EBITDA'; voice-heavy BPO trades at the low end of that band because 'multiples for commoditized delivery models are under pressure' amid AI uncertainty (Site Selection Group). Median for a single-location/small bureau set at 4.0x normalized EBITDA (market-rate owner comp replaced; small-bureau financials are typically SDE - owner salary at a ~$120-150K ops-executive rate deducted before margining).

exit_mult.source: Jahani & Associates digital-CX BPO transaction study; public comps (Teleperformance, Concentrix)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Digital-CX-driven BPO transactions: 'EV/EBITDA multiples average 14x with a median of 13x... from a low of 6x to a high of 22x' - but that cohort is tech-forward CX; voice-heavy public comps price the melt: 'Teleperformance trades at 0.8x EV/Revenue and 4.3x EV/EBITDA' and Concentrix buybacks at 'no more than 5x EBITDA' (multiples.vc; MarketScreener). A $10M+ EBITDA voice-heavy domestic platform realistically exits between the public-comp floor (~4.5x) and the digital-CX low end (~6x+): 6.5x used.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly per-unit labor billing: per agent-hour / per FTE per month for dedicated programs (the majority of outsourced revenue), per-minute or per-call/per-transaction for shared and overflow work, contingency percentage on collections, and per-lead/per-sale on some outbound. Outcome/resolution-based pricing is emerging under gen AI but remains a small minority of contracts. Under every dominant model the billed unit is human time or a human-handled interaction.
pricing_structure.source: Outsource Consultants - pricing in call center/BPO M&A; Auxo Capital Advisors BPO M&A 2025
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: BPO pricing anchors on per-hour/per-FTE/per-transaction contract rates; Auxo: 'The anchor is EV/EBITDA for contract-heavy operators with stable GP%' with buyer objection 'AI will compress your volume/margins' and sellers urged to show 'pricing policy and gainshare guardrails' - direct evidence the default contract passes AI gains to clients absent explicit gainshare terms.

C.arithmetic: C = 10 x pi_dist x pi_moat = 10 x 0.25 x 0.35 = 0.875

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.30945 implies ~30.9pp of revenue in AI-substitutable labor cost - far above the 3pp floor - and A = 6.67, so the inconsistency trigger (uplift < 3pp with A >= 5) does not fire. The tension here is the reverse: the uplift is enormous but under per-unit billing it accrues largely to clients as fewer billed units, which is carried in C (0.875), not suppressed in V.

terminal_value.justification: The product is human conversation sold by the hour or seat; no license, statute, or physical act separates the client from an AI voice agent doing the same job, so the AI agent is the direct replacement of the product the operator sells - not a tool that cuts its delivery cost. Sizing the residual: escalation, empathy-critical, and regulated-disclosure calls plausibly preserve a minority of today's volume (deflection already 45%+ on routine queries and rising with capability), and per-unit pricing on that residual may even rise (complex-call premiums), but the billed-unit base shrinks structurally. Domestic bureaus already lost the cost-arbitrage war to offshore once; AI is a second, deeper cost substitute. Public voice-heavy comps at ~4.3-5x EV/EBITDA are the market pricing this melt. Classified MELTING.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built from the provided role_mix plus deflection-rate and deployment evidence (Gartner-cited 45%+ deflection, production growth data); A from adoption statistics and the CCaaS/IVR analog. No deal activity, consolidation wave, or PE interest was used for V or A; consolidation evidence informed only B (CFD count) and M (multiples).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is predominantly per agent-hour / per FTE / per minute / per transaction, with contingency collections and per-lead outbound - the billed unit is human time. AI efficiency therefore flows to clients as fewer billed units and lower rebid rates, not to the operator as margin; outcome-based re-pricing with gainshare is nascent and a small contract minority. This is the central mechanism of the industry and is reflected directly in pi_dist = 0.25 (deflation of the revenue unit) and reinforced in pi_moat = 0.35 (clients can license AI agents from CCaaS vendors and bypass the bureau entirely).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 10.0 (t50=1) to 3.33 (t50=3); S ranges ~4.08 to ~3.27 around the 3.758 base

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: buy 3-5x, exit 5-9x; M from ~0 (exit 5 / buy 5) to 8.0 (exit 9 / buy 3)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: M=1 gives S ~3.13; M=8 gives S ~4.74; M near 0 collapses S toward 0 - the single most score-sensitive input

reviewer_note: Formula tension, reported per rule 1: S = 3.76 emerges because V saturates at 10 (30.9pp of revenue in substitutable labor) and A is high - but this is precisely the profile of a MELTING industry where the AI value accrues to clients, not the operator. The multiplicative model lets a 0.875 C only partially offset V=10, A=6.67; my judgment is that the composite overstates roll-up attractiveness for a terminal-MELTING code, but the arithmetic is reported as computed. Melting-base caveat (per prompt): n_total is only 2,299 firms and the 201-firm band sits in the 100-499 class where AI-driven volume loss hits hardest; QCEW/SUSB trend verification was not independently completed, so the acquisition universe may be shrinking faster than the 2022 SUSB snapshot implies - treat TD as a ceiling. role_mix is a 4-digit 5614 proxy: medical transcriptionists, mail clerks and counter clerks are sibling-code artifacts; per prompt guidance the large unlisted tail (0.4171 wage share) was treated as predominantly frontline-agent labor, which is the biggest single lever in V (though V stays saturated at 10 across the tail's full 0.4-0.65 range). Offshore substitution is a confounder: domestic revenue can melt via offshoring as well as AI; the MELTING classification rests on AI replacing the product, with offshore as an accelerant. owners_60plus_pct and buy/exit multiples are ESTIMATE-quality (no industry-specific owner-age data exists; small-bureau transaction comps are thin and voice-heavy platform exits are re-pricing downward amid AI uncertainty - public comps at 4.3-5x bound the exit assumption). Adoption surveys mostly cover in-house centers and enterprise buyers rather than BPO operators (prompt trap); firm-count weighting was applied to correct. Consolidation evidence used only for B and M per rule 2. EBITDA quirks (client concentration, facility leases, attrition costs) argue for quality-of-earnings discipline beyond the multiple.
